title: Hypothermia
steps: Move to shelter, out of wind
Remove wet clothing
Insulate from ground (foam pad, branches, pack)
Warm core first: warm drinks (NOT alcohol), body-to-body contact
Do NOT rub extremities — rewarming too fast can cause cardiac arrest
If unconscious but breathing: recovery position, keep warm
If not breathing: CPR and evacuate immediately
doNot: Do NOT give alcohol
Do NOT rub or massage extremities
Do NOT use direct heat on skin
Do NOT let casualty walk if severely hypothermic

title: Heat Stroke
steps: Call for emergency evacuation — this is a medical emergency
Move to shade or coolest available area
Remove excess clothing
Cool aggressively: douse with cold water, fan vigorously
Apply ice packs to neck, armpits, and groin
If conscious and able to swallow, give cool water in small sips
Monitor breathing — be ready for CPR
Continue cooling until body temperature drops below 39°C/102°F
doNot: Do NOT give aspirin or acetaminophen
Do NOT force fluids if unconscious
Do NOT delay cooling to measure temperature

title: Heat Exhaustion
steps: Stop all activity and rest in cool shade
Lie down and elevate legs slightly
Remove excess clothing and loosen remaining clothing
Cool skin with wet cloths or fanning
Sip cool water or electrolyte drink slowly
If no improvement within 30 minutes, treat as heat stroke
Do not resume activity for the rest of the day
doNot: Do NOT give salt tablets
Do NOT resume activity even if feeling better
Do NOT leave unmonitored — can progress to heat stroke

title: Dehydration
symptoms: Dark yellow urine or reduced output
steps: Rest in shade and reduce activity
Sip water or oral rehydration solution frequently (small amounts)
If ORS unavailable: 1 liter water + 6 tsp sugar + ½ tsp salt
Avoid caffeine and alcohol
Eat salty snacks if available to replace electrolytes
Monitor urine color — aim for pale yellow
If unable to keep fluids down or symptoms worsen, evacuate for IV fluids
doNot: Do NOT gulp large amounts at once — sip steadily
Do NOT give caffeine or alcohol
Do NOT ignore dark urine — it is an early warning sign

title: Snake Bite
steps: Move away from the snake — do not attempt to catch or kill it
Keep victim calm and still to slow venom spread
Remove jewelry and tight clothing near bite before swelling
Immobilize bitten limb at or below heart level
Mark the edge of swelling with pen and note time
Photograph the snake if safely possible for identification
Evacuate to medical facility immediately — antivenom may be needed
Monitor for anaphylaxis
doNot: Do NOT apply tourniquet
Do NOT cut the wound or try to suck out venom
Do NOT apply ice
Do NOT give aspirin or ibuprofen (increases bleeding)
Do NOT let victim walk if bite is on leg

title: Bone Fracture
symptoms: Inability to bear weight or use limb
steps: Do not move the injured limb unnecessarily
Check circulation below fracture: pulse, sensation, skin color
Splint the injury in the position found — immobilize joints above and below
Use padding between splint and skin
Apply cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling (20 min on, 20 off)
Elevate if possible without causing pain
Give pain relief if available (ibuprofen or acetaminophen)
Evacuate — all suspected fractures need X-ray
doNot: Do NOT try to realign the bone
Do NOT remove boots if ankle/foot fracture suspected (acts as splint)
Do NOT apply heat

title: Sprain (RICE Protocol)
symptoms: Popping sensation at time of injury
steps: Rest — stop activity and protect the joint
Ice — apply cold pack for 20 minutes every 2 hours
Compression — wrap with elastic bandage (snug but not tight)
Elevation — raise injured area above heart level
Check toes/fingers for circulation (warmth, color, sensation)
Take ibuprofen for pain and inflammation
If unable to bear weight after 24 hours, suspect fracture and evacuate
doNot: Do NOT apply heat in first 48 hours
Do NOT wrap so tightly it cuts circulation
Do NOT walk on severely sprained ankle without support

title: Blister Care
steps: If unbroken and small: protect with moleskin donut or blister pad
If large and painful: sterilize a needle with alcohol or flame
Puncture at the edge, press fluid out gently, leave skin intact
Clean with antiseptic and apply antibiotic ointment
Cover with sterile gauze and secure with tape
Change dressing daily or when wet/dirty
Address the cause: adjust boots, add liner socks, reduce friction
doNot: Do NOT tear off blister skin — it protects the wound
Do NOT pop blisters if you lack sterile supplies
Do NOT ignore hot spots — treat before blisters form

title: Allergic Reaction / Anaphylaxis
steps: Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately if available — inject into outer thigh
Call for emergency evacuation
Lay person flat with legs elevated (unless breathing is difficult — then sit upright)
Remove the trigger if possible (e.g., remove stinger)
Give antihistamine (diphenhydramine/Benadryl) if able to swallow
Be prepared to give second EpiPen dose after 5-15 minutes if no improvement
Monitor airway — be ready for CPR
Even if symptoms improve, evacuate — biphasic reactions can occur hours later
doNot: Do NOT delay epinephrine — antihistamines alone cannot stop anaphylaxis
Do NOT let person stand or sit up suddenly
Do NOT assume improvement means the danger is over

title: Altitude Sickness (AMS)
steps: Stop ascending immediately
Rest at current altitude for at least 24 hours
Hydrate well and eat light, high-carbohydrate meals
Take acetazolamide (Diamox) if available: 125-250mg twice daily
Take ibuprofen or acetaminophen for headache
If symptoms worsen or do not improve in 24 hours: DESCEND at least 500-1000m
If signs of HACE (ataxia, confusion) or HAPE (breathlessness at rest, cough): descend immediately
Administer supplemental oxygen or use portable hyperbaric bag if available
doNot: Do NOT continue ascending with symptoms
Do NOT leave a symptomatic person alone
Do NOT use sleeping pills — they can mask worsening symptoms
Do NOT ignore ataxia (inability to walk heel-to-toe) — this signals HACE

title: Lightning Safety & Strike
steps: PREVENTION: If thunder follows lightning by < 30 seconds, seek shelter immediately
Lightning position: crouch on balls of feet, heels together, head tucked, ears covered
If struck: check for pulse and breathing immediately
Begin CPR if no pulse — lightning victims can often be resuscitated
Treat burns with cool water and sterile dressings
Check for spinal injury before moving
Monitor for delayed cardiac arrhythmias — evacuate all strike victims
Triage: treat apparently dead first (unlike normal triage) — they may recover with CPR
doNot: Do NOT shelter under isolated tall trees
Do NOT lie flat on the ground
Do NOT assume a lightning victim is dead — begin CPR immediately
Do NOT hold metal objects during a storm

title: Jellyfish Sting
steps: Exit the water carefully
Rinse with vinegar (acetic acid) for at least 30 seconds to deactivate unfired stingers
If vinegar unavailable, rinse with sea water (NOT fresh water)
Remove visible tentacles with tweezers or gloved hand — never bare skin
Immerse in hot water (42-45°C/108-113°F) for 20-45 minutes for pain relief
Take antihistamine and pain medication as needed
For box jellyfish or Irukandji: call emergency services immediately
Monitor for allergic reaction
doNot: Do NOT rinse with fresh water — triggers more stinging
Do NOT urinate on the sting (myth, ineffective)
Do NOT scrape with credit card (can trigger unfired stingers)
Do NOT apply ice directly

title: Coral Cuts & Abrasions
symptoms: Laceration or scrape with embedded debris
steps: Clean wound thoroughly with clean fresh water and mild soap
Remove all coral fragments with tweezers and fine brush
Flush with diluted hydrogen peroxide or betadine solution
Apply thin layer of antibiotic ointment
Cover with non-stick sterile dressing
Change dressing twice daily and watch for infection signs
Coral cuts heal slowly — keep clean and dry between dressing changes
Seek medical attention if red streaking, pus, or fever develop
doNot: Do NOT ignore small coral cuts — they commonly become infected
Do NOT seal wound with butterfly strips (traps debris)
Do NOT go back in the water until wound is sealed

title: Severe Bleeding Control
symptoms: Blood spurting or flowing steadily from wound
steps: Apply direct pressure with cleanest available cloth — press hard
If bleeding through pad, add more material on top — do not remove first pad
Elevate the wound above heart level if possible
For limb wounds not controlled by pressure: apply tourniquet 5-8cm above wound
Note time tourniquet applied — write on skin or tape
For junctional wounds (groin, armpit, neck): pack wound tightly with gauze and maintain pressure
Treat for shock: lay flat, elevate legs, keep warm
Evacuate urgently
doNot: Do NOT remove a blood-soaked dressing — add on top
Do NOT loosen or remove tourniquet once applied in field
Do NOT use tourniquet on neck or torso wounds

title: Burns
steps: Remove the source: extinguish flames, remove from heat, remove clothing unless stuck to burn
Cool burn with cool running water for 20 minutes (ideal) — NOT ice-cold
Remove jewelry and non-adhered clothing from burned area before swelling
Cover with clean non-stick dressing or cling film laid along (not wrapped around) the burn
For large burns: wrap in clean sheet, treat for shock, keep warm
Give pain relief and sip fluids if conscious
For chemical burns: brush off dry chemicals, then flush with copious water for 20+ minutes
Evacuate for burns larger than palm size, on face/hands/feet/genitals, or circumferential
doNot: Do NOT apply butter, toothpaste, or creams
Do NOT break blisters
Do NOT use ice or ice water
Do NOT remove clothing stuck to a burn
Do NOT wrap cling film circumferentially

title: Drowning / CPR
steps: Ensure scene safety — do not become a second victim. Use reach/throw/row/go approach
Remove victim from water supporting head and neck (assume spinal injury if diving/surfing)
Check responsiveness: tap shoulders and shout
If not breathing: open airway (head-tilt chin-lift), give 5 rescue breaths
If no pulse: begin CPR — 30 compressions: 2 breaths at 100-120/min
Do NOT attempt abdominal thrusts to clear water from lungs
Use AED if available — dry chest first
Continue CPR until breathing resumes, help arrives, or you are physically unable
All near-drowning victims must be evacuated — delayed pulmonary edema can occur
doNot: Do NOT enter dangerous water without flotation
Do NOT attempt Heimlich to expel water
Do NOT give up CPR early — cold-water drowning victims can recover after prolonged CPR

title: Bee / Wasp Sting
symptoms: In allergic individuals: hives, swelling away from sting, breathing difficulty
steps: Remove stinger quickly by scraping sideways with a flat edge (credit card, knife)
Wash area with soap and water
Apply cold pack for 10-15 minutes to reduce swelling
Take antihistamine (diphenhydramine/cetirizine) to reduce itching and swelling
Take ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain
Apply hydrocortisone cream or calamine lotion if available
Watch for anaphylaxis signs for at least 30 minutes
If ANY systemic reaction: use EpiPen and evacuate (see Anaphylaxis protocol)
doNot: Do NOT squeeze stinger with tweezers (forces more venom in)
Do NOT scratch the sting
Do NOT ignore systemic symptoms in anyone — first-time anaphylaxis is possible

title: Tick Removal
symptoms: Fever or flu-like symptoms days later
steps: Use fine-tipped tweezers — grasp tick as close to skin surface as possible
Pull upward with steady, even pressure — do not twist or jerk
If mouthparts remain, try to remove with tweezers; if not, leave them and let skin heal
Clean bite area with rubbing alcohol or soap and water
Save the tick in a sealed container (tape it to paper or put in small bag with date)
Monitor for symptoms for 30 days: rash, fever, joint pain, fatigue
Seek medical attention if bull's-eye rash appears or flu-like symptoms develop
Document bite location and date
doNot: Do NOT use petroleum jelly, nail polish, or heat to detach tick
Do NOT crush tick with fingers (can spread pathogens)
Do NOT twist or jerk when removing

title: Foreign Body in Eye
steps: Do NOT rub the eye
Wash hands before touching around eye
Try to flush with clean water or saline — tilt head so affected eye is down
Pull upper lid over lower lid — lashes may sweep particle out
If visible on white of eye: try to remove with moist corner of clean cloth
If under upper lid: look down, flip lid over cotton swab, remove if visible
If embedded or on cornea (colored part): do NOT attempt removal — patch and evacuate
After removal: artificial tears and monitor for 24 hours for infection signs
doNot: Do NOT rub the eye
Do NOT try to remove embedded objects
Do NOT touch the cornea
Do NOT use dirty water or cloths

title: Dental Emergency
steps: KNOCKED-OUT TOOTH: Handle by crown only, never touch the root
Rinse gently with milk or saline — do not scrub
Attempt to reimplant in socket: bite gently on gauze to hold in place
If cannot reimplant: store in milk, saliva, or saline (NOT water)
Time critical — best if reimplanted within 30 minutes
BROKEN TOOTH: Rinse mouth with warm water, apply cold compress to face
TOOTHACHE: Rinse with warm salt water, take ibuprofen, apply clove oil if available
Cover sharp edges with dental wax, sugar-free gum, or candle wax to protect tongue/cheek
Seek dental care as soon as possible
doNot: Do NOT handle knocked-out tooth by root
Do NOT store tooth in plain water
Do NOT apply aspirin directly to gums (causes chemical burn)
Do NOT ignore dental infections — they can become systemic
